metadata.title: It's Not the AI Draining the Battery. It's the Radio.

beats.narration_text: Halo, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. The team optimized the neural network for weeks and the battery life barely moved — because inference was never what was eating it.

props.command: Why did optimizing my embedded neural network for weeks not improve battery life on my IoT sensor?
output: LoRa TX: 35 mA × 100 ms = 3.5 mA·s. Inference: 15 mA × 22 ms = 0.33 mA·s. Radio is 10×.

beats.narration_text: This is the book's battery-life curve — inference period on the x-axis, months on the y-axis. See where the curve flattens? That's where the radio becomes the ceiling, not inference.

Start with the baseline: sleep current. The microcontroller in deep sleep draws about 5 microamps — essentially zero on the trace. This is the floor the device returns to between events. Every other activity is a spike above this line.

Inference: the model runs. Current spikes to 15 milliamps for 22 milliseconds, then returns to sleep. Energy: 0.33 milliamp-seconds per inference. Even 1,000 inferences a day is 330 milliamp-seconds. The spike is real — but narrow. It looks dramatic on the trace and is small in the energy budget.

Now the radio. A single LoRa transmission: 35 to 40 milliamps for 100 milliseconds. Energy: 3.5 milliamp-seconds. That is ten times the cost of one inference, from a single transmit event. On the trace, the radio envelope dominates — tall and wide, dwarfing everything that came before. Ten transmissions a day: 35 milliamp-seconds. One thousand inferences: 330 milliamp-seconds. At that ratio, the radio wins the budget.

The comparison bar. Inference energy per day — even at 1,000 runs: 330 milliamp-seconds. Radio energy per day at 10 transmissions: 350 milliamp-seconds, already larger. At 100 transmissions: 3,500 milliamp-seconds. The model is not the bottleneck. The transmit schedule is. Optimize that first.

Your turn. Paste this into Claude: My IoT device runs inference n-inferences times per day — inference-mA milliamps, inference-ms milliseconds each — and transmits n-tx times per day — tx-mA milliamps, tx-ms milliseconds each. Sleep current is sleep-uA microamps, battery is mAh milliamp-hours. Walk me through: one, inference energy budget per day; two, radio energy budget per day; three, which dominates; four, how to re-optimize my transmit schedule to double battery life. Plug in your real sensor numbers.

props.command: My IoT device runs inference [n_inferences] times per day ([inference_mA] mA, [inference_ms] ms each) and transmits [n_tx] times per day ([tx_mA] mA, [tx_ms] ms each). Sleep current is [sleep_uA] µA, battery is [mAh] mAh. Walk me through: (1) inference energy budget per day, (2) radio energy budget per day, (3) which dominates, (4) how to re-optimize transmit schedule to double battery life.

props.title: It's Not the AI Draining the Battery. It's the Radio.
props.subline: embedded ai · liam, in for bear
